title: Branching, Loops, and Conditional Execution
summary: ARM uses branch instructions to implement loops, if-else logic, and function calls. The CMP instruction updates the APSR flags, which conditional branches read to decide whether to jump.
content_html:
Labels and the Program Counter

Every instruction occupies a word in memory and has an address. Labels give meaningful names to those addresses. The Program Counter (PC = R15) holds the address of the next instruction to execute.

MOV R0, #0 Loop: ; label = address of ADD instruction ADD R0, R0, #1 B Loop ; branch to Loop forever HALT

Unconditional Branch

B someplace ; jump to label 'someplace' unconditionally

Conditional Branch — CMP and APSR

CMP subtracts its second operand from the first and updates four flag bits in the Application Program Status Register (APSR):

FlagSet when
NResult was Negative
ZResult was Zero
CCarry out of the MSB
VOverflow (signed arithmetic)

Conditional Branch Instructions

InstructionConditionFlags used
BAlways (unconditional)—
BEQBranch if EqualZ=1
BNEBranch if Not EqualZ=0
BGTBranch if Greater Than (signed)Z=0, N=V
BLTBranch if Less Than (signed)N≠V
BGEBranch if Greater or EqualN=V
BLEBranch if Less or EqualZ=1 or N≠V

Implementing if-else in ARM

; if (x <= 2): x = 0 else: x = 3 LDR R0, X ; load x CMP R0, #2 ; compare x with 2 BGT else ; if R0 > 2, jump to else MOV R0, #0 ; case: x <= 2 B cont ; skip else block else: MOV R0, #3 ; case: x > 2 cont: ... ; continue from here

Implementing a Loop (Busy Wait Timer)

MOV R2, #5000000 ; set iteration count MOV R3, R2 ; copy to working register timer: SUB R3, R3, #1 ; decrement counter CMP R3, #0 ; compare with 0 BNE timer ; loop until R3 == 0
Better timer: A busy-wait loop tied to iteration count depends on CPU speed. Use the hardware .Time register for CPU-independent delays: measure elapsed seconds instead.

exercises.title: Branch instruction
exercises.prompt: B Loop in ARM assembly means:
exercises.options_json: ["Branch only if zero", "Unconditional jump to label Loop", "Compare and branch", "Branch if greater"]

exercises.title: CMP instruction
exercises.options_json: ["R1 + R2 and stores result", "R1 - R2 and updates APSR flags", "R1 AND R2", "R1 OR R2"]
exercises.answer: R1 - R2 and updates APSR flags

exercises.title: Z flag
exercises.prompt: The Z (Zero) flag in APSR is set when:
exercises.options_json: ["Result is negative", "Result is zero", "Carry occurred", "Overflow occurred"]

exercises.title: BGT usage
exercises.prompt: BGT someplace branches to someplace when:
exercises.options_json: ["The previous comparison result was zero", "The previous comparison result was greater than zero", "The previous comparison result was negative", "The carry flag is set"]
exercises.answer: The previous comparison result was greater than zero

exercises.title: Program Counter
exercises.prompt: The Program Counter (PC) in ARM is:

exercises.title: Label purpose
exercises.options_json: ["Defines a variable value", "Names a memory address (of an instruction or data)", "Creates a subroutine with arguments", "Allocates a memory block"]
exercises.answer: Names a memory address (of an instruction or data)

exercises.title: Busy wait issue
exercises.prompt: A busy-wait timer using a countdown loop has what limitation?
exercises.options_json: ["Cannot count to large numbers", "Delay depends on CPU speed, not real time", "Uses too many registers", "Overflows the stack"]
exercises.answer: Delay depends on CPU speed, not real time

exercises.title: BNE in timer
exercises.prompt: In the loop: SUB R3,R3,#1; CMP R3,#0; BNE timer — BNE branches:
exercises.options_json: ["Always", "Only when R3 becomes zero", "While R3 is not zero", "Once only"]

exercises.prompt: The N flag in APSR indicates:
